  • Zhai et al. [2021] Shuangfei Zhai, Walter Talbott, Nitish Srivastava, Chen Huang, Hanlin Goh, Ruixiang Zhang, and Josh Susskind. An attention free transformer. arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.14103, 2021.
  • Zhu et al. [2021] Chen Zhu, Wei Ping, Chaowei Xiao, Mohammad Shoeybi, Tom Goldstein, Anima Anandkumar, and Bryan Catanzaro. Long-short transformer: Efficient transformers for language and vision. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 34, 2021.

Appendix A Related Work

IO-Aware Runtime Optimization. The broad concept of optimizing for reading and writing to fast/slow memory has a long history in computer science and has been known by many names. We draw the most direct connection to the literature of analyzing I/O complexity in this work [1], but concepts of memory hierarchies are fundamental and has appeared in many forms, from the working set model [21], to data locality [86], to the Roofline model of arithmetic intensity [85], to analyses of scalability [59], to standard textbook treatments of computer architecture [40]. We hope that this work encourages the community to adopt these ideas in more parts of the deep learning stack.

Efficient ML Models with Structured Matrices. Matrix multiply is the core computational bottleneck of most machine learning models. To reduce the computational complexity, there have been numerous approaches to learn over a more efficient set of matrices. These matrices are called structured matrices, which have subquadratic (o​(n2)𝑜superscript𝑛2o(n^{2}) for dimension n×n𝑛𝑛n\times n) number of parameters and runtime. Most common examples of structured matrices are sparse and low-rank matrices, along with fast transforms commonly encountered in signal processing (Fourier, Chebyshev, sine/cosine, orthogonal polynomials). There have been several more general classes of structured matrices proposed in machine learning: Toeplitz-like [78], low-displacement rank [49], quasi-separable [25]). The butterfly pattern we use for our block-sparse attention is motivated by the fact that butterfly matrices [64, 15] and their products have been shown to be able to express any structured matrices with almost optimal runtime and number of parameters [20, 16]. However, even though structured matrices are efficient in theory, they have not seen wide adoption since it is hard to translate their efficiency to wall-clock speedup since dense unconstrained matrix multiply has very optimize implementation, a phenomenon known as the hardware lottery [41]. Extensions of butterfly matrices [17, 18] aimed to make butterfly matrices more hardware-friendly.

Sparse Training. Our block-sparse FlashAttention can be seen as a step towards making sparse model training more efficient. Sparse models have seen success in compressing models for inference (pruning) by sparsifying the weight matrices [39, 38, 76, 55, 23]. For model training, the lottery tickets hypothesis [28, 29, 30] suggests that there are a set of small sub-networks derived from a larger dense network that performs as well as the original dense network. Out block-sparse FlashAttention can also be seen as a fixed lottery ticket in the context of attention: we fix the sparsity pattern to be the butterfly pattern through training, and observe that it performs almost as well as the (dense) FlashAttention on the Long-range Arena tasks.

Efficient Transformer. Transformer-based models have become the most widely-used architecture in natural language processing [22] and computer vision [24, 91]. However, one of their computational bottlenecks is that their time and memory scales quadratic in the sequence length. There are numerous approaches to overcome this bottleneck, including approximation with hashing (i.e., sparse) such as Reformer [51] and Smyrf [19] and with low-rank approximation such as Performer [12, 54]. One can even combine sparse and low-rank approximation for better accuracy (e.g., Longformer [3], BigBird [92], Scatterbrain [9], Long-short transformer [94], Combiner [73]). Other approaches include compressing along the sequence dimension to attend to multiple tokens at once [89, 79, 52, 57]. One can also attend over the states from previous sequences to help lengthen the context (e.g., Transformer-XL [14] and Compressive Transformer [69]). We recommend the survey [81] for more details.

There are several lines of work on developing other modules instead of attention to model longer context. HiPPO [35] and its extensions, most notably S4 [36, 37, 31] projects the history on a polynomial basis, allowing accurate reconstruction of the history through state-space models. They combine the strengths of CNNs (efficient training), RNNs (efficient inference), and continuous models (robust to change in sampling rates). LambdaNetworks [2], AFT [93] and FLASH [42] are other attempts at replacing attention in the context of image classification and language modeling.

Appendix B Algorithm Details

We first derive the forward and backward passes of attention and show that they can be computed in a memory-efficient manner (requiring extra memory linear instead of quadratic in the sequence length). Though they reduce the amount of extra memory required, naively they still incur quadratic HBM accesses, resulting in slower execution speed. We describe the FlashAttention algorithm to implement both the forward and the backward passes on GPUs that reduces HBM accesses, leading to both faster runtime and smaller memory footprint.

B.1 Memory-efficient forward pass

The main challenge in making attention memory-efficient is the softmax that couples the columns of 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K} (and columns of 𝐕𝐕\mathbf{V}). Our approach is to compute the softmax normalization constant separately to decouple the columns. This technique [60] has been used in the literature [51, 66] to show that attention computation does not need quadratic extra memory (though the number of HBM accesses is still quadratic, resulting in slow run-time).

For simplicity, we omit here the max-shifting step during softmax. The full algorithm in Section B.3 contains all the steps.

Recall that given input sequences 𝐐,𝐊,𝐕∈ℝN×d𝐐𝐊𝐕superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, we want to compute the attention output 𝐎∈ℝN×d𝐎superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}:

𝐒=𝐐𝐊⊤∈ℝN×N,𝐏=softmax​(𝐒)∈ℝN×N,𝐎=𝐏𝐕∈ℝN×d.formulae-sequence𝐒superscript𝐐𝐊topsuperscriptℝ𝑁𝑁𝐏softmax𝐒superscriptℝ𝑁𝑁𝐎𝐏𝐕superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{S}=\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{P}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S})\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{O}=\mathbf{P}\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}.

We have that Si​j=qiT​kjsubscript𝑆𝑖𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗S_{ij}=q_{i}^{T}k_{j} where qisubscript𝑞𝑖q_{i} and kjsubscript𝑘𝑗k_{j} are the i𝑖i-th and j𝑗j-th columns of 𝐐𝐐\mathbf{Q} and 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K} respectively. Define the normalization constants of softmax:

Li=∑jeqiT​kj.subscript𝐿𝑖subscript𝑗superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗L_{i}=\sum_{j}e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}. (1)

Let vjsubscript𝑣𝑗v_{j} be the j𝑗j-th column of 𝐕𝐕\mathbf{V}, then the i𝑖i-th columns of the output is

oi=Pi:​𝐕=∑jPi​j​vj=∑jeqiT​kjLi​vj.subscript𝑜𝑖subscript𝑃:𝑖absent𝐕subscript𝑗subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗subscript𝑣𝑗subscript𝑗superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝐿𝑖subscript𝑣𝑗o_{i}=P_{i:}\mathbf{V}=\sum_{j}P_{ij}v_{j}=\sum_{j}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}v_{j}. (2)

We see that once Lisubscript𝐿𝑖L_{i} is computed, we can compute oisubscript𝑜𝑖o_{i} without extra memory by repeatedly summing eqiT​kjLi​vjsuperscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝐿𝑖subscript𝑣𝑗\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}v_{j}. Therefore the forward pass can be computed with O​(n)𝑂𝑛O(n) extra memory:

  1. 1.

    Compute Lisubscript𝐿𝑖L_{i} for all i𝑖i according to Eq. 1, which takes O​(n)𝑂𝑛O(n) extra memory.

  2. 2.

    Compute oisubscript𝑜𝑖o_{i} for all i𝑖i according to Eq. 2, which takes O​(d)𝑂𝑑O(d) extra memory.

B.2 Memory-efficient backward pass

We derive the backward pass of attention and show that it can also be computed with linear memory. Rabe and Staats [66] suggests that the backward pass can be done without quadratic extra memory by applying gradient checkpointing to the memory-efficient forward pass. We instead derive the backward pass explicitly and show how it can be computed in a memory-efficient manner.

Suppose that there is a scalar loss function ϕitalic-ϕ\phi, and let the output gradient be 𝐝𝐎∈ℝn×d𝐝𝐎superscriptℝ𝑛𝑑\mathbf{dO}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d} (where 𝐝𝐎𝐝𝐎\mathbf{dO} denotes ∂ϕ∂𝐎italic-ϕ𝐎\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial\mathbf{O}}). We want to compute the input gradients 𝐝𝐐,𝐝𝐊,𝐝𝐕∈ℝn×d𝐝𝐐𝐝𝐊𝐝𝐕superscriptℝ𝑛𝑑\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d} (where 𝐝𝐐,𝐝𝐊,𝐝𝐕𝐝𝐐𝐝𝐊𝐝𝐕\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV} denote ∂ϕ∂𝐐,∂ϕ∂𝐊,∂ϕ∂𝐕italic-ϕ𝐐italic-ϕ𝐊italic-ϕ𝐕\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial\mathbf{Q}},\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial\mathbf{K}},\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial\mathbf{V}} respectively).

The gradient 𝐝𝐕𝐝𝐕\mathbf{dV} is easy to see. Applying reverse-mode autodiff by hand (aka the chain rule), we obtain (in matrix notation) 𝐝𝐕=𝐏T​𝐝𝐎𝐝𝐕superscript𝐏𝑇𝐝𝐎\mathbf{dV}=\mathbf{P}^{T}\mathbf{dO}. Thus:

d​vj=∑iPi​j​d​oi=∑ieqiT​kjLi​d​oi.𝑑subscript𝑣𝑗subscript𝑖subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗𝑑subscript𝑜𝑖subscript𝑖superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝐿𝑖𝑑subscript𝑜𝑖dv_{j}=\sum_{i}P_{ij}do_{i}=\sum_{i}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}do_{i}. (3)

Since we already computed Lisubscript𝐿𝑖L_{i}, d​vj𝑑subscript𝑣𝑗dv_{j} can be computed without extra memory by repeated summing.

The gradients 𝐝𝐐𝐝𝐐\mathbf{dQ} and 𝐝𝐊𝐝𝐊\mathbf{dK} are a little more complicated. We go through the gradients 𝐝𝐏𝐝𝐏\mathbf{dP} and 𝐝𝐒𝐝𝐒\mathbf{dS} first. From Eq. 2, we have that 𝐝𝐏=𝐝𝐎𝐕T𝐝𝐏superscript𝐝𝐎𝐕𝑇\mathbf{dP}=\mathbf{dO}\mathbf{V}^{T}, and so:

d​Pi​j=d​oiT​vj.𝑑subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑇subscript𝑣𝑗dP_{ij}=do_{i}^{T}v_{j}.

Recall that Pi:=softmax​(Si:)subscript𝑃:𝑖absentsoftmaxsubscript𝑆:𝑖absentP_{i:}=\mathrm{softmax}(S_{i:}). Using the fact that the Jacobian of y=softmax​(x)𝑦softmax𝑥y=\mathrm{softmax}(x) is diag​(y)−y​yTdiag𝑦𝑦superscript𝑦𝑇\mathrm{diag}(y)-yy^{T}, we have that

d​Si:=(diag​(Pi:)−Pi:​Pi:T)​d​Pi:=Pi:∘d​Pi:−(Pi:T​d​Pi:)​Pi:,𝑑subscript𝑆:𝑖absentdiagsubscript𝑃:𝑖absentsubscript𝑃:𝑖absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑃:𝑖absent𝑇𝑑subscript𝑃:𝑖absentsubscript𝑃:𝑖absent𝑑subscript𝑃:𝑖absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑃:𝑖absent𝑇𝑑subscript𝑃:𝑖absentsubscript𝑃:𝑖absentdS_{i:}=(\mathrm{diag}(P_{i:})-P_{i:}P_{i:}^{T})dP_{i:}=P_{i:}\circ dP_{i:}-(P_{i:}^{T}dP_{i:})P_{i:},

where ∘\circ denotes pointwise multiplication.

Define

Di=Pi:T​d​Pi:=∑jeqiT​kjLi​d​oiT​vj=d​oiT​∑jeqi⊤​kjLi​vj=d​oiT​oi,subscript𝐷𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑃:𝑖absent𝑇𝑑subscript𝑃:𝑖absentsubscript𝑗superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝐿𝑖𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑇subscript𝑣𝑗𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑇subscript𝑗superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖topsubscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝐿𝑖subscript𝑣𝑗𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑇subscript𝑜𝑖D_{i}=P_{i:}^{T}dP_{i:}=\sum_{j}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}do_{i}^{T}v_{j}=do_{i}^{T}\sum_{j}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{\top}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}v_{j}=do_{i}^{T}o_{i}, (4)

then

d​Si:=Pi:∘d​Pi:−Di​Pi:.𝑑subscript𝑆:𝑖absentsubscript𝑃:𝑖absent𝑑subscript𝑃:𝑖absentsubscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑃:𝑖absentdS_{i:}=P_{i:}\circ dP_{i:}-D_{i}P_{i:}.

Hence

d​Si​j=Pi​j​d​Pi​j−Di​Pi​j=Pi​j​(d​Pi​j−Di).𝑑subscript𝑆𝑖𝑗subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗𝑑subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗subscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗𝑑subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗subscript𝐷𝑖dS_{ij}=P_{ij}dP_{ij}-D_{i}P_{ij}=P_{ij}(dP_{ij}-D_{i}).

Now we can get the gradients 𝐝𝐐𝐝𝐐\mathbf{dQ} and 𝐝𝐊𝐝𝐊\mathbf{dK}. Recall that Si​j=qiT​kjsubscript𝑆𝑖𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗S_{ij}=q_{i}^{T}k_{j}, so

d​qi=∑jd​Si​j​kj=∑jPi​j​(d​Pi​j−Di)​kj=∑jeqiT​kjLi​(d​oiT​vj−Di)​kj.𝑑subscript𝑞𝑖subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑆𝑖𝑗subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝑗subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗𝑑subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗subscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝑗superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝐿𝑖𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑇subscript𝑣𝑗subscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑘𝑗dq_{i}=\sum_{j}dS_{ij}k_{j}=\sum_{j}P_{ij}(dP_{ij}-D_{i})k_{j}=\sum_{j}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}(do_{i}^{T}v_{j}-D_{i})k_{j}. (5)

Similarly,

d​kj=∑id​Si​j​qi=∑iPi​j​(d​Pi​j−Di)​qi=∑ieqiT​kjLi​(d​oiT​vj−Di)​qi.𝑑subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝑖𝑑subscript𝑆𝑖𝑗subscript𝑞𝑖subscript𝑖subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗𝑑subscript𝑃𝑖𝑗subscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑞𝑖subscript𝑖superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖𝑇subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝐿𝑖𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑇subscript𝑣𝑗subscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑞𝑖dk_{j}=\sum_{i}dS_{ij}q_{i}=\sum_{i}P_{ij}(dP_{ij}-D_{i})q_{i}=\sum_{i}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}(do_{i}^{T}v_{j}-D_{i})q_{i}. (6)

Therefore the backward pass can also be computed with O​(n)𝑂𝑛O(n) extra memory:

  1. 1.

    Compute d​vj𝑑subscript𝑣𝑗dv_{j} for all j𝑗j according to Eq. 3, which takes O​(d)𝑂𝑑O(d) extra memory.

  2. 2.

    Compute Disubscript𝐷𝑖D_{i} for all i𝑖i according to Eq. 4, which takes O​(n)𝑂𝑛O(n) extra memory.

  3. 3.

    Compute d​qi𝑑subscript𝑞𝑖dq_{i} for all i𝑖i according to Eq. 5, which takes O​(d)𝑂𝑑O(d) extra memory.

  4. 4.

    Compute d​kj𝑑subscript𝑘𝑗dk_{j} for all j𝑗j according to Eq. 6, which takes O​(d)𝑂𝑑O(d) extra memory.

B.3 FlashAttention: Forward Pass

We describe the full details of FlashAttention forward pass. Given input sequences 𝐐,𝐊,𝐕∈ℝN×d𝐐𝐊𝐕superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, we want to compute the attention output 𝐎∈ℝN×d𝐎superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}:

𝐒=τ​𝐐𝐊⊤∈ℝN×N,𝐒masked=mask​(S)∈ℝN×N,𝐏=softmax​(𝐒masked)∈ℝN×N,formulae-sequence𝐒𝜏superscript𝐐𝐊topsuperscriptℝ𝑁𝑁superscript𝐒maskedmask𝑆superscriptℝ𝑁𝑁𝐏softmaxsuperscript𝐒maskedsuperscriptℝ𝑁𝑁\displaystyle\mathbf{S}=\tau\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{S}^{\mathrm{masked}}=\textsc{mask}(S)\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{P}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S}^{\mathrm{masked}})\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},
𝐏dropped=dropout​(𝐏,pdrop),𝐎=𝐏dropped​𝐕∈ℝN×d,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝐏droppeddropout𝐏subscript𝑝drop𝐎superscript𝐏dropped𝐕superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\displaystyle\mathbf{P}^{\mathrm{dropped}}=\mathrm{dropout}(\mathbf{P},p_{\mathrm{drop}}),\quad\mathbf{O}=\mathbf{P}^{\mathrm{dropped}}\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d},

where τ∈ℝ𝜏ℝ\tau\in\mathbb{R} is some softmax scaling (typically 1d1𝑑\frac{1}{\sqrt{d}}), mask is some masking function that sets some entries of the input to −∞-\infty and keep other entries the same (e.g., key padding mask when sequences in the batch don’t have the same lengths and are padded), and dropout​(x,p)dropout𝑥𝑝\mathrm{dropout}(x,p) applies dropout to x𝑥x elementwise (i.e., output x1−p𝑥1𝑝\frac{x}{1-p} with probability 1−p1𝑝1-p and output 0 with probability p𝑝p for each element x𝑥x).

The full algorithm is in Algorithm 2. We save the output 𝐎𝐎\mathbf{O}, the softmax statistics ℓℓ\ell and m𝑚m, and the pseudo-random number generator state ℛℛ{\cal R} for the backward pass.

B.4 FlashAttention: Backward Pass

We describe the full details of FlashAttention backward pass. Given input sequences 𝐐,𝐊,𝐕∈ℝN×d𝐐𝐊𝐕superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, the output 𝐎∈ℝN×d𝐎superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, and the output gradient 𝐝𝐎𝐝𝐎\mathbf{dO}, we want to compute the input gradients 𝐝𝐐,𝐝𝐊,𝐝𝐕∈ℝN×d𝐝𝐐𝐝𝐊𝐝𝐕superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}.

We first describe the standard attention backward pass in Algorithm 3 for completeness.

We now make two observations about FlashAttention backward pass:

  1. 1.

    We do not need to store the dropout mask of size O​(N2)𝑂superscript𝑁2O(N^{2}) from the forward pass. Instead, we can save the pseudo-random number generator states from the forward pass and re-generate the dropout mask in the backward pass. This allows us to only use O​(N)𝑂𝑁O(N) extra memory.

  2. 2.

    When computing the softmax gradient, we use Eq. 4 to compute Di=Pi:⊤​d​Pi:subscript𝐷𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑃:𝑖absenttop𝑑subscript𝑃:𝑖absentD_{i}=P_{i:}^{\top}dP_{i:} without reducing over Pi:subscript𝑃:𝑖absentP_{i:} and d​Pi:𝑑subscript𝑃:𝑖absentdP_{i:} of size N𝑁N (they might not fit into SRAM). Instead we can rewrite Di=d​oi⊤​oisubscript𝐷𝑖𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖topsubscript𝑜𝑖D_{i}=do_{i}^{\top}o_{i} and compute the dot product between vectors of size d𝑑d.

The full FlashAttention backward pass algorithm is in Algorithm 4. Conceptually it is just a block version of the derivation in Section B.2.

We see that similar to the forward pass, the backward pass performs O​(N2)𝑂superscript𝑁2O(N^{2}) FLOPs and only requires O​(N)𝑂𝑁O(N) extra memory beyond inputs, output, output gradient, and input gradients.

We analyze the IO-complexity of the backward pass, similar to the forward pass (Theorem 2).

Theorem 5.

Let N𝑁N be the sequence length, d𝑑d be the head dimension, and M𝑀M be size of SRAM with d≤M≤N​d𝑑𝑀𝑁𝑑d\leq M\leq Nd. Standard attention (Algorithm ) backward pass requires Θ​(N​d+N2)Θ𝑁𝑑superscript𝑁2\Theta(Nd+N^{2}) HBM accesses, while FlashAttention backward pass (Algorithm 4) requires Θ​(N2​d2​M−1)Θsuperscript𝑁2superscript𝑑2superscript𝑀1\Theta(N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1}) HBM accesses.

The proof is in Appendix C.

B.5 Comparison with Rabe and Staats [66]

We describe here some similarities and differences between our FlashAttention algorithm and the algorithm of Rabe and Staats [66].

Conceptually, both FlashAttention and Rabe and Staats [66] operate on blocks of the attention matrix using the well-established technique of tiling (or softmax scaling) [60, 51]. To reduce the memory footprint, both methods avoid storing the large attention matrix in the forward pass and recompute it in the backward pass.

The first major difference is that Rabe and Staats [66] focuses on the reducing the total memory footprint (maximum amount of GPU memory required) while FlashAttention focuses on reducing memory accesses (the number of memory reads/writes). As mentioned in Section 2, the amount of memory access is the primary determining factor of runtime. Reducing memory accesses also necessarily reduces the total amount of memory required (e.g., if an operation incurs A𝐴A memory accesses, then its total memory requirement is at most A𝐴A). As a result, FlashAttention is faster than standard attention (2-4×\times) while Rabe and Staats [66] is around the same speed or slightly slower than standard attention. In terms of total memory required, both methods offer substantial memory saving.

The second difference between the two methods is the way information is summarized from each block to pass to the next block. Rabe and Staats [66] summarizes each block with its temporary output along with the softmax normalization statistics. At the end of the forward pass, the temporary outputs of all the blocks are combined using the statistics to produce the final output. FlashAttention instead incrementally updates the output (Algorithm 1 line 12) after processing each block, so only one copy of the output is needed (instead of K𝐾K copies for K𝐾K blocks). This means that FlashAttention has smaller total memory requirement compared to Rabe and Staats [66].

The final major difference is the way the backward pass is computed. Rabe and Staats [66] uses gradient checkpointing to recompute the attention matrix and the temporary output of each block. FlashAttention instead simplifies the backward pass analytically (Sections B.2 and B.4). It only recomputes the attention matrix and does not recompute the temporary output of each block. This reduces the memory requirement for the backward pass and yields speedup.

Appendix C Proofs

Proof of Theorem 1.

We first count the number of FLOPs and extra memory required.

The dominating FLOPs are from matrix multiplication. In the inner loop, (Algorithm 1 line 9), we compute 𝐐i​𝐊j⊤∈ℝBr×Bcsubscript𝐐𝑖superscriptsubscript𝐊𝑗topsuperscriptℝsubscript𝐵𝑟subscript𝐵𝑐\mathbf{Q}_{i}\mathbf{K}_{j}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}} for 𝐐i∈ℝBr×dsubscript𝐐𝑖superscriptℝsubscript𝐵𝑟𝑑\mathbf{Q}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times d} and 𝐊j∈ℝBc×dsubscript𝐊𝑗superscriptℝsubscript𝐵𝑐𝑑\mathbf{K}_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{c}\times d}, which takes O​(Br​Bc​d)𝑂subscript𝐵𝑟subscript𝐵𝑐𝑑O(B_{r}B_{c}d) FLOPs. We also compute (Algorithm 1 line 12) 𝐏~i​j​𝐕j∈ℝBr×dsubscript~𝐏𝑖𝑗subscript𝐕𝑗superscriptℝsubscript𝐵𝑟𝑑\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij}\mathbf{V}_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times d} for 𝐏~i​j∈ℝBr×Bcsubscript~𝐏𝑖𝑗superscriptℝsubscript𝐵𝑟subscript𝐵𝑐\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}} and 𝐕j∈ℝBc×dsubscript𝐕𝑗superscriptℝsubscript𝐵𝑐𝑑\mathbf{V}_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{c}\times d}, which takes O​(Br​Bc​d)𝑂subscript𝐵𝑟subscript𝐵𝑐𝑑O(B_{r}B_{c}d) FLOPs. We execute the inner loops Tc​Tr=⌈NBc⌉​⌈NBr⌉subscript𝑇𝑐subscript𝑇𝑟𝑁subscript𝐵𝑐𝑁subscript𝐵𝑟T_{c}T_{r}=\left\lceil\frac{N}{B_{c}}\right\rceil\left\lceil\frac{N}{B_{r}}\right\rceil times. Therefore the total number of FLOPs is

O​(N2Bc​Br​Br​Bc​d)=O​(N2​d).𝑂superscript𝑁2subscript𝐵𝑐subscript𝐵𝑟subscript𝐵𝑟subscript𝐵𝑐𝑑𝑂superscript𝑁2𝑑O\left(\frac{N^{2}}{B_{c}B_{r}}B_{r}B_{c}d\right)=O(N^{2}d).

In terms of extra memory required, we see that we need O​(N)𝑂𝑁O(N) memory to store the statistics (ℓ,m)ℓ𝑚(\ell,m).

We now prove the algorithm’s correctness by induction on j𝑗j for 0≤j≤Tc0𝑗subscript𝑇𝑐0\leq j\leq T_{c}. Let 𝐊:j∈ℝj​Bc×dsubscript𝐊:absent𝑗superscriptℝ𝑗subscript𝐵𝑐𝑑\mathbf{K}_{:j}\in\mathbb{R}^{jB_{c}\times d} be the first j​Bc𝑗subscript𝐵𝑐jB_{c} rows of 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K}, and similarly 𝐕:j∈ℝj​Bc×dsubscript𝐕:absent𝑗superscriptℝ𝑗subscript𝐵𝑐𝑑\mathbf{V}_{:j}\in\mathbb{R}^{jB_{c}\times d} the the first j​Bc𝑗subscript𝐵𝑐jB_{c} rows of 𝐕𝐕\mathbf{V}. Let 𝐒:,:j=𝐐𝐊:j⊤∈ℝN×j​Bcsubscript𝐒::absent𝑗superscriptsubscript𝐐𝐊:absent𝑗topsuperscriptℝ𝑁𝑗subscript𝐵𝑐\mathbf{S}_{:,:j}=\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}_{:j}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times jB_{c}}, and 𝐏:,:j=softmax​(𝐒:,:j)∈ℝN×j​Bcsubscript𝐏::absent𝑗softmaxsubscript𝐒::absent𝑗superscriptℝ𝑁𝑗subscript𝐵𝑐\mathbf{P}_{:,:j}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S}_{:,:j})\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times jB_{c}} (softmax applied row-wise). Let mj,ℓ(j),𝐎(j)superscript𝑚𝑗superscriptℓ𝑗superscript𝐎𝑗m^{j},\ell^{(j)},\mathbf{O}^{(j)} be the values of m,ℓ,𝐎𝑚ℓ𝐎m,\ell,\mathbf{O} in HBM after the j𝑗j-th iteration of the outer loop (Algorithm 1 line 5). (Note that these values of m,ℓ,𝐎𝑚ℓ𝐎m,\ell,\mathbf{O} are updated after each iteration of the outer loop.) We want to show that after the j𝑗j-th iteration of the outer loop, we have computed in HBM:

m(j)=rowmax​(𝐒:,:j)∈ℝN,ℓ(j)=rowsum​(exp⁡(𝐒:,:j−m(j)))∈ℝN,𝐎(j)=𝐏:,:j​𝐕:j∈ℝN×d.formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑚𝑗rowmaxsubscript𝐒::absent𝑗superscriptℝ𝑁superscriptℓ𝑗rowsumsubscript𝐒::absent𝑗superscript𝑚𝑗superscriptℝ𝑁superscript𝐎𝑗subscript𝐏::absent𝑗subscript𝐕:absent𝑗superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑m^{(j)}=\mathrm{rowmax}(\mathbf{S}_{:,:j})\in\mathbb{R}^{N},\quad\ell^{(j)}=\mathrm{rowsum}(\exp(\mathbf{S}_{:,:j}-m^{(j)}))\in\mathbb{R}^{N},\quad\mathbf{O}^{(j)}=\mathbf{P}_{:,:j}\mathbf{V}_{:j}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}.

Based on our initialization (Algorithm 1 line 2), this claim is true for j=0𝑗0j=0 (i.e., before the any iteration of the outer loop is executed). Suppose that the claim holds for some j=0,…,Tc−1𝑗0…subscript𝑇𝑐1j=0,\dots,T_{c}-1. We want to show that the claim also holds for j+1𝑗1j+1. Indeed, when we update the statistics in the inner loop (Algorithm 1 line 10) on the (j+1)𝑗1(j+1)-th iteration of the outer loop, we update m(j+1)=max⁡(m(j),m~)superscript𝑚𝑗1superscript𝑚𝑗~𝑚m^{(j+1)}=\max(m^{(j)},\tilde{m}) where m~∈ℝN~𝑚superscriptℝ𝑁\tilde{m}\in\mathbb{R}^{N} is the row-max of 𝐒:,j:j+1subscript𝐒::𝑗𝑗1\mathbf{S}_{:,j:j+1}, the slice of 𝐒𝐒\mathbf{S} from column j​Bc𝑗subscript𝐵𝑐jB_{c} to column (j+1)​Bc−1𝑗1subscript𝐵𝑐1(j+1)B_{c}-1. This implies that

m(j+1)=rowmax​(𝐒:,:j+1)∈ℝN.superscript𝑚𝑗1rowmaxsubscript𝐒::absent𝑗1superscriptℝ𝑁m^{(j+1)}=\mathrm{rowmax}(\mathbf{S}_{:,:j+1})\in\mathbb{R}^{N}.

Similarly, we update

ℓ(j+1)=em(j)−m(j+1)​ℓ(j)+em~−m(j+1)​ℓ~,superscriptℓ𝑗1superscript𝑒superscript𝑚𝑗superscript𝑚𝑗1superscriptℓ𝑗superscript𝑒~𝑚superscript𝑚𝑗1~ℓ\ell^{(j+1)}=e^{m^{(j)}-m^{(j+1)}}\ell^{(j)}+e^{\tilde{m}-m^{(j+1)}}\tilde{\ell},

where ℓ~=rowsum​(exp⁡(𝐒:,j:j+1−m~))∈ℝN~ℓrowsumsubscript𝐒::𝑗𝑗1~𝑚superscriptℝ𝑁\tilde{\ell}=\mathrm{rowsum}(\exp(\mathbf{S}_{:,j:j+1}-\tilde{m}))\in\mathbb{R}^{N}. By the same algebraic manipulation in Section 3.1, we obtain:

ℓ(j+1)=rowsum​(exp⁡(𝐒:,:j+1−m(j+1)))∈ℝN.superscriptℓ𝑗1rowsumsubscript𝐒::absent𝑗1superscript𝑚𝑗1superscriptℝ𝑁\ell^{(j+1)}=\mathrm{rowsum}(\exp(\mathbf{S}_{:,:j+1}-m^{(j+1)}))\in\mathbb{R}^{N}.

Let 𝐕j:j+1subscript𝐕:𝑗𝑗1\mathbf{V}_{j:j+1} be the slice of 𝐕𝐕\mathbf{V} from column j​Bc𝑗subscript𝐵𝑐jB_{c} to column (j+1)​Bc−1𝑗1subscript𝐵𝑐1(j+1)B_{c}-1, we also update:

𝐎(j+1)superscript𝐎𝑗1\displaystyle\mathbf{O}^{(j+1)} =diag​(ℓ(j+1))−1​(diag​(ℓ(j))​em(j)−m(j+1)​𝐎(j)+em~−m(j+1)​exp⁡(𝐒j:j+1−m~)​𝐕j:j+1)absentdiagsuperscriptsuperscriptℓ𝑗11diagsuperscriptℓ𝑗superscript𝑒superscript𝑚𝑗superscript𝑚𝑗1superscript𝐎𝑗superscript𝑒~𝑚superscript𝑚𝑗1subscript𝐒:𝑗𝑗1~𝑚subscript𝐕:𝑗𝑗1\displaystyle=\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j+1)})^{-1}(\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j)})e^{m^{(j)}-m^{(j+1)}}\mathbf{O}^{(j)}+e^{\tilde{m}-m^{(j+1)}}\exp(\mathbf{S}_{j:j+1}-\tilde{m})\mathbf{V}_{j:j+1})
=diag​(ℓ(j+1))−1​(diag​(ℓ(j))​em(j)−m(j+1)​𝐏:,:j​𝐕:j+e−m(j+1)​exp⁡(𝐒j:j+1)​𝐕j:j+1)absentdiagsuperscriptsuperscriptℓ𝑗11diagsuperscriptℓ𝑗superscript𝑒superscript𝑚𝑗superscript𝑚𝑗1subscript𝐏::absent𝑗subscript𝐕:absent𝑗superscript𝑒superscript𝑚𝑗1subscript𝐒:𝑗𝑗1subscript𝐕:𝑗𝑗1\displaystyle=\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j+1)})^{-1}(\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j)})e^{m^{(j)}-m^{(j+1)}}\mathbf{P}_{:,:j}\mathbf{V}_{:j}+e^{-m^{(j+1)}}\exp(\mathbf{S}_{j:j+1})\mathbf{V}_{j:j+1})
=diag​(ℓ(j+1))−1​(diag​(ℓ(j))​em(j)−m(j+1)​diag​(ℓ(j))​exp⁡(𝐒:,:j−m(j))​𝐕:j+e−m(j+1)​exp⁡(𝐒j:j+1)​𝐕j:j+1)absentdiagsuperscriptsuperscriptℓ𝑗11diagsuperscriptℓ𝑗superscript𝑒superscript𝑚𝑗superscript𝑚𝑗1diagsuperscriptℓ𝑗subscript𝐒::absent𝑗superscript𝑚𝑗subscript𝐕:absent𝑗superscript𝑒superscript𝑚𝑗1subscript𝐒:𝑗𝑗1subscript𝐕:𝑗𝑗1\displaystyle=\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j+1)})^{-1}(\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j)})e^{m^{(j)}-m^{(j+1)}}\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j)})\exp(\mathbf{S}_{:,:j}-m^{(j)})\mathbf{V}_{:j}+e^{-m^{(j+1)}}\exp(\mathbf{S}_{j:j+1})\mathbf{V}_{j:j+1})
=diag​(ℓ(j+1))−1​(e−m(j+1)​exp⁡(𝐒:,:j)​𝐕:j+e−m(j+1)​exp⁡(𝐒j:j+1)​𝐕j:j+1)absentdiagsuperscriptsuperscriptℓ𝑗11superscript𝑒superscript𝑚𝑗1subscript𝐒::absent𝑗subscript𝐕:absent𝑗superscript𝑒superscript𝑚𝑗1subscript𝐒:𝑗𝑗1subscript𝐕:𝑗𝑗1\displaystyle=\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j+1)})^{-1}(e^{-m^{(j+1)}}\exp(\mathbf{S}_{:,:j})\mathbf{V}_{:j}+e^{-m^{(j+1)}}\exp(\mathbf{S}_{j:j+1})\mathbf{V}_{j:j+1})
=diag​(ℓ(j+1))−1​(exp⁡(𝐒:,:j−m(j+1))​𝐕:j+exp⁡(𝐒j:j+1−m(j+1))​𝐕j:j+1)absentdiagsuperscriptsuperscriptℓ𝑗11subscript𝐒::absent𝑗superscript𝑚𝑗1subscript𝐕:absent𝑗subscript𝐒:𝑗𝑗1superscript𝑚𝑗1subscript𝐕:𝑗𝑗1\displaystyle=\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j+1)})^{-1}(\exp(\mathbf{S}_{:,:j}-m^{(j+1)})\mathbf{V}_{:j}+\exp(\mathbf{S}_{j:j+1}-m^{(j+1)})\mathbf{V}_{j:j+1})
=diag​(ℓ(j+1))−1​(exp⁡([𝐒:,:j𝐒j:j+1]−m(j+1)))​[𝐕:j𝐕j:j+1]absentdiagsuperscriptsuperscriptℓ𝑗11matrixsubscript𝐒::absent𝑗subscript𝐒:𝑗𝑗1superscript𝑚𝑗1matrixsubscript𝐕:absent𝑗subscript𝐕:𝑗𝑗1\displaystyle=\mathrm{diag}(\ell^{(j+1)})^{-1}\left(\exp\left(\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{S}_{:,:j}&\mathbf{S}_{j:j+1}\end{bmatrix}-m^{(j+1)}\right)\right)\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{V}_{:j}\\ \mathbf{V}_{j:j+1}\end{bmatrix}
=softmax​(𝐒:j+1)​𝐕:j+1.absentsoftmaxsubscript𝐒:absent𝑗1subscript𝐕:absent𝑗1\displaystyle=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S}_{:j+1})\mathbf{V}_{:j+1}.

We then see that the claim is also true for j+1𝑗1j+1. By induction, the claim is true for all j=0,…,Tc𝑗0…subscript𝑇𝑐j=0,\dots,T_{c}.

When j=Tc𝑗subscript𝑇𝑐j=T_{c}, we conclude that the final value of 𝐎𝐎\mathbf{O} in HBM is softmax​(𝐒)​𝐕=softmax​(𝐐𝐊⊤)​𝐕softmax𝐒𝐕softmaxsuperscript𝐐𝐊top𝐕\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S})\mathbf{V}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top})\mathbf{V}.

∎

Proof of Theorem 2.

We first analyze the IO complexity of standard attention implementation. The inputs 𝐐,𝐊,𝐕∈ℝN×d𝐐𝐊𝐕superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} reside in HBM, and the at the end of the algorithm the output 𝐎∈ℝN×d𝐎superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} is written to HBM.

In the first step of computing the matrix multiply 𝐒=𝐐𝐊⊤𝐒superscript𝐐𝐊top\mathbf{S}=\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}, the inputs 𝐐,𝐊𝐐𝐊\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K} are read from HBM and the output 𝐒∈ℝN×N𝐒superscriptℝ𝑁𝑁\mathbf{S}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} is written to HBM (Algorithm  line 1). This incurs Θ​(N​d+N2)Θ𝑁𝑑superscript𝑁2\Theta(Nd+N^{2}) HBM accesses.

In the second step of computing 𝐏=softmax​(𝐒)𝐏softmax𝐒\mathbf{P}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S}), the input 𝐒𝐒\mathbf{S} is read from HBM and the output 𝐏𝐏\mathbf{P} is written to HBM (Algorithm  line 2). This incurs Θ​(N2)Θsuperscript𝑁2\Theta(N^{2}) HBM accesses.

In the last step of computing 𝐎=𝐏𝐕𝐎𝐏𝐕\mathbf{O}=\mathbf{P}\mathbf{V}, the inputs 𝐏,𝐕𝐏𝐕\mathbf{P},\mathbf{V} are read from global memory and the output 𝐎𝐎\mathbf{O} is written to HBM (Algorithm  line 3). This incurs Θ​(N​d+N2)Θ𝑁𝑑superscript𝑁2\Theta(Nd+N^{2}) HBM accesses.

Overall, standard attention implementation requires Θ​(N​d+N2)Θ𝑁𝑑superscript𝑁2\Theta(Nd+N^{2}) global memory accesses.

We now analyze the IO complexity of streaming attention.

Following Algorithm 1, we see that each element of 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K} and 𝐕𝐕\mathbf{V} is loaded from HBM once (Algorithm 1 line 6). We make Tcsubscript𝑇𝑐T_{c} passes over 𝐐𝐐\mathbf{Q} and 𝐎𝐎\mathbf{O}, each pass loading all of 𝐐𝐐\mathbf{Q} and all of 𝐎𝐎\mathbf{O} to HBM (Algorithm 1 line 8). Therefore the number of HBM accesses is Θ​(N​d+N​d​Tc)=Θ​(N​d​Tc)Θ𝑁𝑑𝑁𝑑subscript𝑇𝑐Θ𝑁𝑑subscript𝑇𝑐\Theta\left(Nd+NdT_{c}\right)=\Theta(NdT_{c}).

We derive the conditions on the block sizes Bcsubscript𝐵𝑐B_{c} and Brsubscript𝐵𝑟B_{r}. We need the blocks 𝐊jsubscript𝐊𝑗\mathbf{K}_{j} and 𝐕jsubscript𝐕𝑗\mathbf{V}_{j} of size Bc×dsubscript𝐵𝑐𝑑B_{c}\times d to fit into on-chip memory, which translates to:

Bc​d=O​(M)⇔Bc=O​(Md).⇔subscript𝐵𝑐𝑑𝑂𝑀subscript𝐵𝑐𝑂𝑀𝑑B_{c}d=O(M)\Leftrightarrow B_{c}=O\left(\frac{M}{d}\right).

Similarly, we need the blocks 𝐐i,𝐎isubscript𝐐𝑖subscript𝐎𝑖\mathbf{Q}_{i},\mathbf{O}_{i} of size Br×dsubscript𝐵𝑟𝑑B_{r}\times d to fit into on-chip memory, which translates to:

Br​d=O​(M)⇔Br=O​(Md).⇔subscript𝐵𝑟𝑑𝑂𝑀subscript𝐵𝑟𝑂𝑀𝑑B_{r}d=O(M)\Leftrightarrow B_{r}=O\left(\frac{M}{d}\right).

Finally, we need the block 𝐒i​jsubscript𝐒𝑖𝑗\mathbf{S}_{ij} of size Br×Bcsubscript𝐵𝑟subscript𝐵𝑐B_{r}\times B_{c} to fit into on-chip memory, which translates to:

Br​Bc=O​(M).subscript𝐵𝑟subscript𝐵𝑐𝑂𝑀B_{r}B_{c}=O(M).

We therefore set:

Bc=Θ​(Md),Br=Θ​(min⁡(Md,MBc))=Θ​(min⁡(Md,d)).formulae-sequencesubscript𝐵𝑐Θ𝑀𝑑subscript𝐵𝑟Θ𝑀𝑑𝑀subscript𝐵𝑐Θ𝑀𝑑𝑑B_{c}=\Theta\left(\frac{M}{d}\right),\qquad B_{r}=\Theta\left(\min\left(\frac{M}{d},\frac{M}{B_{c}}\right)\right)=\Theta\left(\min\left(\frac{M}{d},d\right)\right).

We then have:

Tc=NBc=Θ​(N​dM).subscript𝑇𝑐𝑁subscript𝐵𝑐Θ𝑁𝑑𝑀T_{c}=\frac{N}{B_{c}}=\Theta\left(\frac{Nd}{M}\right).

As a result, the number of HBM accesses is:

Θ​(N​d​Tc)=Θ​(N2​d2M).Θ𝑁𝑑subscript𝑇𝑐Θsuperscript𝑁2superscript𝑑2𝑀\Theta\left(NdT_{c}\right)=\Theta\left(\frac{N^{2}d^{2}}{M}\right).

∎

Proof of Proposition 3.

For contradiction, suppose that there exists an algorithm that computes exact attention where the number for HBM access for all M∈[d,N​d]𝑀𝑑𝑁𝑑M\in[d,Nd] is

o​(N2​d2M).𝑜superscript𝑁2superscript𝑑2𝑀o\left(\frac{N^{2}d^{2}}{M}\right).

In the regime of M=Θ​(N​d)𝑀Θ𝑁𝑑M=\Theta(Nd), this results in the number of HBM accesses:

o​(N2​d2N​d)=o​(N​d).𝑜superscript𝑁2superscript𝑑2𝑁𝑑𝑜𝑁𝑑o\left(\frac{N^{2}d^{2}}{Nd}\right)=o(Nd).

However, the input to attention (matrices 𝐐,𝐊,𝐕𝐐𝐊𝐕\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}) and the output 𝐎𝐎\mathbf{O} have size N​d𝑁𝑑Nd and they start out being in HBM, so if the algorithm computes exact attention it must incur at least Ω​(N​d)Ω𝑁𝑑\Omega(Nd) HBM accesses. This is a contradiction. ∎

Proof of Theorem 5.

The IO complexity of the attention backward is very similar to the IO complexity of the attention forward (Theorem 2). Here we provide a sketch of the proof.

We first analyze the IO complexity of standard attention backward pass. The inputs 𝐐,𝐊,𝐕,𝐝𝐎∈ℝN×d𝐐𝐊𝐕𝐝𝐎superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V},\mathbf{dO}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} reside in HBM, and the at the end of the algorithm the outputs 𝐝𝐐,𝐝𝐊,𝐝𝐕∈ℝN×d𝐝𝐐𝐝𝐊𝐝𝐕superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} are written to HBM.

At each step of the standard attention backward pass, one needs to load inputs of size N​d𝑁𝑑Nd or N2superscript𝑁2N^{2} from HBM, and needs to write the outputs of size N2superscript𝑁2N^{2} or N​d𝑁𝑑Nd to HBM. This incurs Θ​(N​d+N2)Θ𝑁𝑑superscript𝑁2\Theta(Nd+N^{2}) HBM accesses.

We now analyze the IO complexity of FlashAttention backward pass.

Similar to Theorem 2, we see that each element of 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K} and 𝐕𝐕\mathbf{V} is loaded from HBM once. Each element of 𝐝𝐊𝐝𝐊\mathbf{dK} and 𝐝𝐕𝐝𝐕\mathbf{dV} is only written to HBM once. We make Tcsubscript𝑇𝑐T_{c} passes over 𝐐,𝐎,𝐝𝐎𝐐𝐎𝐝𝐎\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{O},\mathbf{dO}, each pass loading all of 𝐐,𝐎,𝐝𝐎𝐐𝐎𝐝𝐎\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{O},\mathbf{dO} to HBM. We also make Tcsubscript𝑇𝑐T_{c} passes over 𝐝𝐐𝐝𝐐\mathbf{dQ}, each pass reading/writing all of 𝐝𝐐𝐝𝐐\mathbf{dQ} from/to HBM. Therefore the number of HBM accesses is Θ​(N​d+N​d​Tc)=Θ​(N​d​Tc)Θ𝑁𝑑𝑁𝑑subscript𝑇𝑐Θ𝑁𝑑subscript𝑇𝑐\Theta\left(Nd+NdT_{c}\right)=\Theta(NdT_{c}).

As in the proof of Theorem 2, the constraints on the block sizes are that:

Bc=Θ​(Md),Br=Θ​(min⁡(Md,d)).formulae-sequencesubscript𝐵𝑐Θ𝑀𝑑subscript𝐵𝑟Θ𝑀𝑑𝑑B_{c}=\Theta\left(\frac{M}{d}\right),\qquad B_{r}=\Theta\left(\min\left(\frac{M}{d},d\right)\right).

We then have:

Tc=NBc=Θ​(N​dM).subscript𝑇𝑐𝑁subscript𝐵𝑐Θ𝑁𝑑𝑀T_{c}=\frac{N}{B_{c}}=\Theta\left(\frac{Nd}{M}\right).

As a result, the number of HBM accesses is:

Θ​(N​d​Tc)=Θ​(N2​d2M).Θ𝑁𝑑subscript𝑇𝑐Θsuperscript𝑁2superscript𝑑2𝑀\Theta\left(NdT_{c}\right)=\Theta\left(\frac{N^{2}d^{2}}{M}\right).

∎

Appendix D Extension Details

D.1 Block-sparse FlashAttention

We describe the full block-sparse FlashAttention algorithm in Algorithm 5. The algorithm is identical to Algorithm 2, except that we skip zero blocks.

We prove the IO-complexity of block-sparse FlashAttention.

Proof of Proposition 4.

The proof is very similar to the proof of Theorem 2. For the block-sparse case, notice that we only need to load blocks corresponding to nonzero blocks. As a result, the number of HBM accesses are scaled by s𝑠s, the fraction of nonzero blocks in the block-sparsity mask. However, for small values of s𝑠s, we would still need to write the result 𝐎∈ℝN×d𝐎superscriptℝ𝑁𝑑\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}. Therefore the number of HBM accesses is

Θ​(N​d+N2​d2M​s).Θ𝑁𝑑superscript𝑁2superscript𝑑2𝑀𝑠\Theta\left(Nd+\frac{N^{2}d^{2}}{M}s\right).

∎

D.2 Potential Extensions

We discuss here a few potential extensions of the IO-aware approach to speed up deep learning training.

Multi-GPU Attention. Large language models are trained on hundreds or thousands of GPUs, and one typically splits the attention computation between 4-8 GPUs on the same node [77]. This introduces another level of memory hierarchy: beside GPU SRAM and GPU HBM, we also have the HBM of other GPUs. For very long sequences, the different GPUs on the same node can cooperate to compute attention by taking into account the asymmetry of different levels of memory hierarchy.

Sparse MLP layers. Typical dense MLP layers are compute-bound and not memory-bound. To improve their efficiency, MLP layers with sparse weight matrices can be used [17]. However, many sparse MLP layers are instead memory-bound, and their speedup is often not proportional to the sparsity. We believe that an IO-aware implementation can alleviate this issue and realize the benefits of sparsity. We are excited about future work in this direction, to reduce the computational requirement of large models and improve their wall-block runtime.

Kernel machine learning. Our approach in FlashAttention relies on the fact that the N×N𝑁𝑁N\times N attention matrix is a function of a low-rank matrix 𝐐𝐊⊤superscript𝐐𝐊top\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top} (of rank d≪Nmuch-less-than𝑑𝑁d\ll N). As a result, we can repeatedly load the inputs 𝐐,𝐊𝐐𝐊\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K} and recompute the block of the attention matrix that we need, significantly reducing HBM access. As similar scenario happens in kernel machine learning: each element Ki​jsubscript𝐾𝑖𝑗K_{ij} of the N×N𝑁𝑁N\times N kernel matrix 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K} is a function of two vectors of size d≪Nmuch-less-than𝑑𝑁d\ll N, as it measures the similarity between two datapoints xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i} and xjsubscript𝑥𝑗x_{j}. The KeOps library [26, 8] is a successful example of how reducing memory reads/writes can speed up kernel operations. We hope that this will motivate kernel methods that focus more on reducing IOs instead of just FLOPs.

Appendix E Full Experimental Results

E.1 BERT

We train BERT-large following the training procedure and hyperparameters of the reference MLPerf 1.1 implementation. In particular, we use the LAMB optimizer with learning rate 3.75e-3, with batch size 448, trained for at most 7100 steps. The training is stopped once the validation accuracy (for masked language modeling) reaches the target 72.0%, and the wall-clock run-time is measured. We train with FP16 precision using Apex AMP (with O2 optimization level).

We compare our results with the reported training speed from Nvidia that was submitted to MLPerf 1.1 (Table 1).

We use the same train / validation data split provided by MLPerf 1.1 reference implementation. In particular, we evaluate on the same 10000 validation examples as the baseline from Nvidia.

We train the model on 8×\timesA100-80GB GPUs. Each training run takes between 16 and 19 minutes, and we average the results of 10 runs.

E.2 GPT-2

We use the standard implementations of GPT-2 [67] from Huggingface transformers library and from Nvidia’s Megatron-LM repo. We follow the training recipe of the Megatron-LM repo.

We use an effective batch size of 512, and use gradient accumulation to fit into available GPU memory. We use the AdamW optimizer, with learning rate 6e-4 for GPT-2 small and 1.5e-4 for GPT-2 medium, and weight decay of 0.1. All models are trained with the same hyperparameters for 400K steps. We run all implementations with mixed-precision training (PyTorch AMP).

We use the Openwebtext dataset, with the GPT-2 BPE tokenizer. We randomly select 0.5% of the dataset as the validation set, with the rest being used as training set. This random selection of validation set is done once, and all models are evaluated on the same validation set.

We train the model on 8×\timesA100-40GB GPUs, and we measure the wall-clock training time. Training GPT-2 small takes between 2.7-9.5 days, and training GPT-2 medium takes between 6.9-21.0 days (Table 2).

In Fig. 4, we plot of the validation perplexity throughout training of GPT-2 small/medium, using either HuggingFace implementation or our FlashAttention implementation. We see that FlashAttention behaves the same as the baseline implementation and the validation perplexity curves of the two implementations almost lie on top of each other.

Long Document Classification.

For MIMIC-III and ECtHR, we follow the hyperparameters of Dai et al. [13].

E.3 LRA details

We follow the hyperparameters from the Long-range arena paper [80], the Long-range arena repo (https://github.com/google-research/long-range-arena), and the Nyströmformer reproduction [90]. To be generous to the baseline methods, if we are unable to reproduce the performance of any baseline for any of the five tasks, we report the better performance from Tay et al. [80] or Xiong et al. [90] for that baseline on that task.

After hyperparameter tuning, almost all of the attention methods achieve similar accuracy on all of the five LRA tasks.

We run all methods with mixed-precision training, except for Performer (not stable with mixed precision) and Local Attention (implementation does not support FP16).

To calculate the overall wallclock-time speedup, we take the geometric mean of the wallclock-time speedup of each of the five tasks.

Path-X

For Path-X and Path-256, we follow the hyperparameters from the PathFinder-32 experiments from the long-range arena paper[80]. For both, we first pretrain a model on Path-64. We take the checkpoint after 200 epochs, upsample its positional embedding (we duplicate the positional embeddings gridwise in space), and fine-tune it on the downstream task for 200 epochs with one epoch of linear warmup, and cosine decay of the learning rate. For Path-X, we take the best performing checkpoint (according to val accuracy), and additionally fine-tune it for 200 epochs with the same warmup and learning rate (this adds roughly 4 points of accuracy to FlashAttention for Path-X, but the model starts overfitting afterwards).

E.4 Comparison with Apex FMHA

We compare our method/implementation with Apex FMHA (https://github.com/NVIDIA/apex/tree/master/apex/contrib/csrc/fmha).

When we started this project, Apex FMHA was the fastest implementation of attention (that we knew of), tailored for short sequences of length at most 512. In fact, almost all MLPerf submissions for BERT training benchmark running on Nvidia GPUs use FMHA for their model code, as of MLPerf 1.1 [58]. Since FMHA targets BERT models, it only supports head dimension 64, and only runs on A100 GPUs. FMHA fuses the attention computation dropout​(softmax​(mask​(𝐐𝐊⊤)))​𝐕dropoutsoftmaxmasksuperscript𝐐𝐊top𝐕\mathrm{dropout}(\mathrm{softmax}(\textsc{mask}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top})))\mathbf{V} into one CUDA kernel. In the forward pass, it stores the attention matrix softmax​(mask​(𝐐𝐊T))softmaxmasksuperscript𝐐𝐊𝑇\mathrm{softmax}(\textsc{mask}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{T})) to HBM to be used in gradient computation. As a result, it does not offer substantial memory saving (though for shorter sequences memory footprint is often not a primary concern).

We use FMHA code as a starting point, and apply two well-established techniques (tiling and recomputation) to deal with long sequences and to save memory as mentioned in Section 3. As a result, we can support much longer sequences (e.g., up to length 64K). We also support more head dimensions (16, 32, 64, 128) and broader GPU types (all Turing and Ampere GPUs at the time of writing).

In Table 7, we compare the performance of FlashAttention and Apex FMHA for short sequences (as FMHA only supports sequence length at most 512). Generally FlashAttention is slightly faster than FMHA in the forward pass and slightly slower than FMHA in the backward pass. This is because we do not store the attention matrix in the forward pass and recompute it in the backward pass. Compared to FMHA, the overall runtime of FlashAttention is about 4% slower for sequence length 128, 8% faster for sequence length 256, and 5% faster for sequence length 512.

E.5 Speedup On Different Hardware and Configurations

Speedup varies between different types of GPU types and generations depending on HBM bandwidth and SRAM size. In this section, we profile FlashAttention speedup on different GPUs and configurations.

A100

Figure 5 shows speedup on an A100 GPU with batch size 8, head dimension 64, and 12 attention heads, across different sequence lengths. We generally see 2-4×\times speedup, and we see more speedup when using dropout and masking due to kernel fusion.

A100, Head Dimension 128

Speedup also changes when we increase the head dimension. Each block requires more memory, so we need to use smaller block sizes to fit into SRAM. Figure 6 shows speedup with head dimension 128 on an A100 (batch size 16, 12 heads). We see less speedup overall—but we can still see significant speedup (up to 3×\times) with a causal mask, where half the blocks are masked out.

RTX 3090

Figure 7 shows speedup on an RTX 3090 GPU. Here, we use batch size 12 with 12 attention heads. We observe slightly higher speedups on the RTX 3090 (between 2.5-4.5×\times), since the memory bandwidth on an RTX 3090 is lower than on an A100 (roughly 900 GB/s vs. 1.5 TB/s).

T4

Figure 8 shows speedup on a T4 GPU. T4 SRAM is smaller than A100, so we need to make the block sizes smaller in FlashAttention. As a result, we observe less speedup on T4, which matches the IO complexity analysis in Section 3.2. T4 GPUs are commonly used for inference, so we also report speedup on the forward pass only.

E.6 Full Benchmarking Results

We report the full benchmarking results and experimental details on A100.

Baselines

We compare against reference implementations for exact attention from PyTorch/HuggingFace and Megatron, approximate attention, and sparse attention. For approximate attention, we compare against reference implementations of Reformer [51], Local Attention [68], Linformer Attention [84], Smyrf [19], and LongShortFormer (LSFormer) [94]. For sparse attention, we compare against reference implementations of Block-Sparse Attention form OpenAI [11], Longformer[3], and BigBird Attention [92]. For the approximate and sparse attention, we use a compression ratio of 1/8, or a compressed sequence length of 256, whichever is smaller.

Setup

We measure runtime and memory usage of the attention computation with 8 heads of dimension 64, and batch size 16 on a machine with one A100 GPU with 40 GB of GPU HBM. We vary sequence length in our experiments. We compute attention on random vectors for 𝐐𝐐\mathbf{Q}, 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K}, and 𝐕𝐕\mathbf{V} (we do not measure the projection from the hidden layer). For dropout, we use dropout 0.1; for masking, we use a padding mask with uniformly-random mask lengths between the total sequence length and the total sequence length minus 20. To measure runtime, we take the average of 100 measurements of the attention call. We only measure memory footprint once, since it does not vary between runs.

We report timing results on the forward pass, backward pass, and combined forward + backward pass. We measure each method with and without dropout, masking, or both—except for Block Sparse, Longformer, and BigBird. These methods did not successfully run the backward pass with masking due to a bug in external libraries, so we measured them without masking to be generous. We use FP16 for all measurements, except for Local Attention, whose implementation only supports FP32.

For each baseline, we increase sequence length until it runs out of memory on the GPU, except for the following exceptions: The Megatron implementation does not support sequence lengths longer than 2048. Block-Sparse (OpenAI) does not support sequence lengths longer than 4096. Longformer and BigBird do not support sequence lengths longer than 8092.

We measure memory usage on the combined forward + backward pass, without dropout or masking.

Results

Table 8 summarizes all the experimental configurations and contains pointers to the results tables.
